meta.name: HCL-32 Hypomania Checklist
meta.description: The HCL-32 is a 32-item self-report screening tool developed by Jules Angst et al. to identify hypomanic symptoms and help screen for bipolar spectrum disorders. Think back to a time when you were in a 'high' or 'excited' state.
meta.author: Angst et al. (2005)
meta.reference: Angst J, Adolfsson R, Benazzi F, et al. The HCL-32: Towards a self-assessment tool for hypomanic symptoms in outpatients. J Affect Disord. 2005;88(2):217-233.
meta.instruction: Think of a time when you were in a 'high' or 'excited' state. How did you feel then? Please answer Yes or No for each item.

questions.text: I need less sleep
I feel more energetic and more active
I am more self-confident
I enjoy my work more
I am more sociable (make more phone calls, go out more)
I want to travel and/or do travel more
I tend to drive faster or take more risks when driving
I spend more money / too much money
I take more risks in my daily life (in my work and/or other activities)
I am physically more active (sport etc.)
I plan more activities or projects
I have more ideas, I am more creative
I am less shy or inhibited
I wear more colorful and more extravagant clothes/make-up
I want to meet or actually do meet more people
I am more interested in sex, and/or have increased sexual desire
I am more flirtatious and/or am more sexually active
I talk more
I think faster
I make more jokes or puns when I am talking
I am more easily distracted
I engage in lots of new things
My thoughts jump from topic to topic
I do things more quickly and/or more easily
I am more impatient and/or get irritable more easily
I can be exhausting or irritating for others
I get into more quarrels
My mood is higher, more optimistic
I drink more coffee
I smoke more cigarettes
I drink more alcohol
I take more drugs (sedatives, anti-anxiety pills, stimulants)

ranges.label: Few Hypomanic Features
ranges.description: You reported few hypomanic symptoms, scoring well below the clinical screening threshold of 14. Periods of elevated mood or increased activity have not been a prominent feature of your experience. No particular concern is indicated at this time.
suggestions: No particular concern is indicated at this time based on your screening results
If you experience significant mood fluctuations in the future, consider re-taking this screening
Being aware of your baseline mood patterns can help you recognize meaningful changes early

ranges.label: Some Hypomanic Features
ranges.description: You reported some hypomanic symptoms but scored below the screening threshold of 14. While not screen-positive, this indicates features worth monitoring. Tracking mood patterns over time can help you and your clinician identify meaningful changes.
suggestions: Pay attention to the pattern and duration of mood fluctuations, especially periods of unusually high energy
Keeping a daily mood diary can help you identify cycles and triggers for mood changes
If mood swings begin to affect your work, relationships, or sleep, consider consulting a mental health professional
Learn about the difference between normal mood variation and clinically significant hypomania

ranges.label: Screen Positive
ranges.description: Your score meets or exceeds the screening threshold of 14, suggesting possible bipolar spectrum features. This is a screening result, not a diagnosis. A comprehensive assessment by a psychiatrist is needed to determine if bipolar disorder is present.
suggestions: Consult a psychiatrist or clinical psychologist for a comprehensive diagnostic evaluation
Keep detailed records of your mood patterns, sleep changes, and energy levels to share with your clinician
Learn about bipolar spectrum disorders from reputable sources to understand what this screening result means
Do not self-diagnose or self-medicate based on a screening score alone; professional assessment is essential
If you are currently taking antidepressants, inform your prescriber about this screening result

ranges.label: Prominent Hypomanic Features
ranges.description: You reported many hypomanic symptoms, scoring well above the clinical screening threshold. This strongly suggests bipolar spectrum features requiring professional evaluation. A comprehensive psychiatric assessment is recommended to guide treatment.
suggestions: Strongly recommended to schedule an appointment with a psychiatrist as soon as possible
Document your detailed mood history, including periods of both elevation and depression, for your clinician
Provide your doctor with a complete history of all mood episodes and their impact on your functioning
If you are currently on medication, do not adjust dosage or stop taking it without medical guidance
